question: from traffic metadata alone, which implementation is running?
cv: group-safe 5-fold StratifiedGroupKFold (grouped by prompt_group); macro-F1 with pooled-OOF bootstrap 95% CI
feature_representation: 195-dim per-trace flat vector minus 5 structural-count features → 190-dim (traffic shape).
no_identity_features: TRUE — no feature is derived from a port, IP, hostname, or agent identity; flat_vector() never references flow_key. Port is not used at all in Phase 1 (no per-agent labels).
excluded_rationale: endpoint/flow counts trivially encode structure (the topology baseline). Excluding them makes this a traffic-SHAPE fingerprint.

timing_ablation.note: Re-run with TIMING features ALSO removed, to test whether same-call-structure runtimes (A vs C_langgraph, per §3 'structure-invariant, timing-shifted') separate on TIMING rather than structure.

verdict_phase1: RECON VULNERABILITY CONFIRMED (≥0.90, CI clear of chance)
confound_caveat: READ THIS BEFORE THE NUMBER. macro-F1 is near-perfect (0.998) and EVERY pair separates at ~1.0 — including A vs C_langgraph, which share call structure by design and survive the timing ablation. Perfect separation of things that should be similar is the classic signature of a CAPTURE-SESSION / BATCH confound: each implementation was collected in a SEPARATE session, so a classifier can key on session-specific artefacts (SDK/version byte differences, host state, clock granularity) instead of a genuine implementation fingerprint. Two readings are consistent with the data and we cannot separate them here: (1) genuine — different orchestrator programs (LangGraph vs asyncio) emit different-sized control traffic, a real recon signal; (2) confounded — session/batch artefacts inflate separability. The clean test is a SAME-SESSION INTERLEAVED capture of the implementations (future work). Honest verdict: recon of a STRUCTURALLY-DISTINCT framework (a2a_mcp vs our A2A) is real and expected; the within-family A/B/C magnitudes should be treated as an UPPER BOUND pending the interleaved-capture control.
verdict_note_A_vs_C: A and C_langgraph DO separate (full-shape separability 0.9972), which is NOT the naive 'shared structure → indistinguishable' prediction. Mechanism: the vector includes timing and A/C differ in timing (§3). With timing removed, A↔C separability is 0.9889 — it stays high, so A/C separate on non-timing traffic shape too (report as-is). CAVEAT: A and C were captured in separate sessions, so part of the timing gap could be capture-session artefact rather than a pure runtime signature; a same-session interleaved capture is the clean test (future work).
